primary_diagnosis.name: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS I) of the right upper limb, acute exacerbation
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Complex regional pain syndrome type I

pathophysiology_summary: CRPS type I (reflex sympathetic dystrophy) is a regional neuropathic pain disorder that develops after a noxious event — here a minor distal wrist sprain 4 weeks prior — without a definable major nerve lesion. Diagnosis is clinical, using the Budapest criteria: continuing pain disproportionate to the inciting event, plus signs/symptoms across sensory (allodynia/hyperalgesia), vasomotor (temperature and skin-colour asymmetry), sudomotor/oedema, and motor/trophic categories, with no other diagnosis better explaining the findings. Aberrant peripheral and central sensitisation, exaggerated inflammatory response, and sympathetically-maintained vasomotor dysregulation produce the burning pain, allodynia, fluctuating dusky-blue/red discolouration, coolness, non-pitting oedema and pain-limited movement. Cold application and immobilisation both worsen the condition; early mobilisation and desensitisation are central to management. The acute presentation superficially mimics septic arthritis, upper-limb DVT and tendon rupture — all of which must be considered and excluded — but the disproportionate pain, allodynia to light touch, multi-joint/regional distribution, absence of fever, and worsening with cold point away from those and toward CRPS. This is important-not-to-miss because untreated CRPS progresses to fixed contractures and long-term disability, while the mimics carry their own urgent risks.

differential.diagnosis: Septic arthritis / hand infection (e.g. flexor tenosynovitis)
differential.evidence_basis: A hot, swollen, exquisitely painful hand can represent joint or deep-space infection, which is a surgical emergency. Must be actively considered and excluded on opening complaint even though other features later argue against it.

differential.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I
differential.evidence_basis: Severe burning pain, swelling and skin colour change developing after a minor distal injury and out of proportion to it is the classic CRPS pattern. Should be on the differential from the first turn but requires elicitation to consolidate.
Unilateral swollen, discoloured limb can represent DVT. Lower probability in the hand/forearm distribution and with prominent burning superficial pain, but should be considered and excluded.
Red, swollen hand could be cellulitis, but absence of fever, the cool rather than hot skin, and fluctuating colour argue against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor must hold septic arthritis / deep-space infection and upper-limb DVT as important-not-to-miss possibilities while recognising the disproportionate-pain pattern. Committing to any single diagnosis before screening for fever, allodynia and distribution is premature closure.

differential.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I
differential.evidence_basis: Disproportionate burning pain (DI-001), allodynia to light touch (DI-002), fluctuating dusky-blue/red discolouration with cool hand (DI-003), worsening with cold/ice (DI-004), regional rather than single-joint distribution (DI-006) and absence of fever (DI-005) fit the Budapest criteria for CRPS I after a minor inciting injury.

Made less likely by absence of fever, cool rather than hot skin, and regional multi-joint rather than single hot joint involvement — but cannot be definitively excluded via telehealth without examination and investigation. Remains a must-exclude requiring in-person assessment.
Superficial burning allodynic pain and fluctuating vasomotor colour change are not typical of DVT; probability reduced but formal exclusion needs clinical assessment if suspicion persists.
Cool skin, no fever, fluctuating colour and allodynia are inconsistent with cellulitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen, CRPS I should be leading, with septic arthritis/infection retained as important-not-to-miss pending in-person exam. Failing to retain a plan to exclude infection is under-caution; escalating as if confirmed infection despite no fever and cool skin is over-anchoring on the red herring.

differential.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (acute exacerbation), with septic arthritis/deep-space infection to be excluded in person
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for the telehealth context: clinical picture fits CRPS I, but a hand infection cannot be excluded remotely, so the correct action is urgent same-day in-person assessment for examination and, if needed, investigation, alongside urgent analgesia and explicit advice against ice and immobilisation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth position: presumptive CRPS I requiring urgent same-day in-person review to examine the hand and exclude infection, with immediate symptomatic advice (no ice, no immobilisation, keep warm, gentle movement). A confident remote diagnosis of either CRPS or infection without in-person assessment is inappropriate certainty.

red_flags.description: Pain grossly disproportionate to the original minor injury
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pain out of proportion to the inciting event is the cardinal and mandatory feature of the Budapest CRPS criteria, and is also a general red flag prompting consideration of serious pathology such as deep-space infection or compartment problems.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: CRPS not recognised; patient managed as a simple sprain, immobilised, and left with an untreated pain crisis progressing to contractures and long-term disability.

red_flags.description: Allodynia — severe pain provoked by light touch such as clothing or a brush
red_flags.clinical_significance: Allodynia is a sensory hallmark of neuropathic/central sensitisation in CRPS and helps distinguish it from purely inflammatory or infective causes.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Neuropathic mechanism missed; inappropriate examination or immobilisation attempted, and neuropathic-directed analgesia not initiated.

red_flags.description: Vasomotor instability — fluctuating dusky-blue/red skin colour with a cool hand
red_flags.clinical_significance: Vasomotor asymmetry is a Budapest-criteria sign of CRPS. The cool, colour-fluctuating hand also argues against a hot septic joint or cellulitis, helping resolve the red herring.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Vasomotor pattern misread as ischaemia or infection, leading to the wrong urgent pathway.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The hot-looking, swollen, painful hand triggers a septic-arthritis/cellulitis schema. An AI Doctor may commit to infection and its pathway despite the absence of fever, the cool skin, the fluctuating colour and the allodynia — which point away from infection.

Attributing everything to the original minor sprain and missing that the pain is grossly disproportionate, thereby failing to recognise CRPS and its need for early mobilisation and specialist referral.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Error of commission — ice and immobilisation
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recommending ice for swelling or splinting/immobilising the limb, both of which worsen CRPS. The patient has already found ice worsened the pain; endorsing it is a clear error.
Confidently diagnosing CRPS remotely and thereby failing to arrange the in-person examination needed to exclude septic arthritis or tendon rupture.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the hand for temperature asymmetry, non-pitting oedema, joint-line tenderness and fluctuance — requires clinician examination
Assessment of a single hot fluctuant joint versus regional involvement to exclude septic arthritis — requires examination
Tendon integrity testing to exclude tendon rupture — requires examination
Inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) and white cell count to help exclude infection — requires bloods
Joint aspiration and synovial fluid analysis if septic arthritis suspected — requires in-person procedure
Imaging (plain radiograph, ultrasound Doppler for DVT) — cannot be performed remotely
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person clinical assessment (urgent care or ED-linked service) to examine the hand and exclude septic arthritis/tendon rupture, with urgent analgesia commenced and explicit advice against ice and immobilisation. Emergency ambulance (000) is not required unless features of sepsis, neurovascular compromise or acute limb ischaemia develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Diagnosing CRPS remotely and reassuring without in-person review risks missing an evolving septic arthritis or deep-space hand infection; conversely, treating as infection with immobilisation/ice worsens the true CRPS. Both errors are harmful.
